message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "BEST LOCAL / OPEN-SOURCE TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) MODELS TO REPLACE OR AUGMENT KOKORO for an English educational-podcast pipeline, as of 2026. Baseline to beat: Kokoro-82M (hexgrad/Kokoro-82M), currently run locally on Apple Silicon (CPU + MPS), producing 24kHz audio, multiple voices (af_heart/am_onyx/bf_isabella/bm_george etc.), American + British English, two-voice format (narrator + question voice). Evaluate candidates across these angles: (1) QUALITY/NATURALNESS — which open/local TTS models beat Kokoro on naturalness and intelligibility for English (candidates to assess: XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Kitten-TTS, Zonos, and any newer 2025-2026 open models) — report MOS/quality benchmarks, license (commercial/redistribution OK?), and model size. (2) WORD/PHONEME-LEVEL TIMING & ALIGNMENT — which models natively output word/phoneme timestamps (durations), and what forced-alignment tools (Montreal Forced Aligner, WhisperX, aeneas, ctc-forced-aligner) best produce a text↔audio timing map from existing audio — this enables content-aware speed compression, script-highlighting, and a true text-to-audio map. (3) HIGH-SPEED COMPREHENSIBILITY — which model's output stays most intelligible under aggressive time-compression / at high playback speed (crisp consonants, clean formant transitions); also the best FORMANT/robotic option (eSpeak-NG, RHVoice, DECtalk-like) for an extreme 16x 'ultra' track. (4) MULTI-VOICE & VOICE CONTROL — voice variety, voice cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding new voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format. (5) LOCAL-INFERENCE PRACTICALITY on Apple Silicon and CPU — real-time factor / batch render speed, RAM/VRAM needs, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and integration effort to swap into a Python pipeline that today calls Kokoro. For EACH recommended model give: quality vs Kokoro, license, local-inference feasibility on a Mac, whether it emits alignment, and a clear verdict on whether it's worth switching to (or adding alongside Kokoro). Prefer authoritative/benchmark sources and recent (2025-2026) comparisons; flag where claims are vendor marketing vs independent evaluation."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://github.com/m-bain/whisperX/issues/1247
**Title:** WhisperX word-level timestamps inaccurate compared to Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA) — Issue #1247
**Found via:** alignment/timing — timestamps & forced aligners search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.

- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

input.prompt: Extract the issue title, the original poster's claims, any maintainer responses, and dates. Specifically: What does this issue say about WhisperX word-level timestamp accuracy compared to Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA)? Are there specific accuracy numbers, error magnitudes (e.g. milliseconds of offset), or qualitative comparisons? What workarounds, recommendations, or conclusions are given? Quote relevant passages verbatim. Note the publish date and whether this is a forum/issue discussion.

content.content:
# WhisperX Timestamp Accuracy Issue Analysis

**Issue Title:** "Word-level timestamps from WhisperX are inaccurate compared to Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA) #1247"

**Posted:** October 3, 2025

**Forum Type:** GitHub Issues (open-source project discussion)

## Original Poster's Claims

User @smpa01 reports significant discrepancies between WhisperX and Montreal Forced Aligner outputs. The poster states: "I've noticed that the timestamps generated by WhisperX are significantly off" and provides a comparison table visualization showing "differences in word boundaries between MFA and WhisperX for the same audio."

**Key observation:** "While MFA produces highly accurate word-level timestamps," WhisperX's alignment is substantially less accurate. The poster validated MFA results using Praat audio visualization software.

## Specific Accuracy Data

The content does not provide quantified error margins (milliseconds of offset) or numerical accuracy percentages. The issue relies on visual comparison via an attached image showing the discrepancies.

## Command Used

```
whisperx source.wav --model large-v3 --language en --align_model WAV2VEC2_ASR_LARGE_LV60K_960H --highlight_words True
```

## Workarounds & Status

No maintainer responses, workarounds, or conclusions are present in the provided content. The issue remains open with no assigned resolution.

content.text: The fetch succeeded but returned limited detail. Let me verify by checking the raw issue content via the GitHub API to confirm the claims and look for any additional comments.

input.command: gh api repos/m-bain/whisperX/issues/1247 --jq '{title: .title, created_at: .created_at, state: .state, comments: .comments, body: .body}' 2>/dev/null || echo "GH_API_FAILED"
input.description: Fetch WhisperX issue 1247 via GitHub API

content.content: {"body":"Hi, I’ve been experimenting with [Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA)](https://github.com/MontrealCorpusTools/Montreal-Forced-Aligner) and WhisperX on the same `.wav` audio file to compare word-level timestamp accuracy. While MFA produces highly accurate word-level timestamps, I’ve noticed that the timestamps generated by WhisperX are significantly off. I’ve attached a comparison table (see image) showing the differences in word boundaries between MFA and WhisperX for the same audio. **My WhisperX command:** ```sh whisperx source.wav --model large-v3 --language en --align_model WAV2VEC2_ASR_LARGE_LV60K_960H --highlight_words True ``` **Questions:** - Given forced alignment is an intended use case for WhisperX, am I missing any recommended settings or workflow to improve alignment accuracy? I was expecting WhisperX to generate identical word-level timestamps. I have used Praat to visualize the MFA output and found it to be very accurate. Any guidance or clarification would be greatly appreciated! Thank you. ","comments":5,"created_at":"2025-10-03T20:44:07Z","state":"open","title":"Word-level timestamps from WhisperX are inaccurate compared to Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA)"}

content.text: The API confirms there are 5 comments. Let me fetch them to see if maintainers or others provided quantified data, workarounds, or rebuttals.

I now have the full picture: the original issue plus 5 corroborating comments from multiple independent users, plus a cited Interspeech 2024 paper. This gives strong material for falsifiable claims. Let me compile the structured output.

claims.claim: Multiple independent WhisperX users report that WhisperX's wav2vec2-based forced-alignment word-level timestamps are significantly less accurate than Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA) on the same English audio, with MFA validated as accurate via Praat.
claims.quote: While MFA produces highly accurate word-level timestamps, I've noticed that the timestamps generated by WhisperX are significantly off. I've attached a comparison table (see image) showing the differences in word boundaries between MFA and WhisperX for the same audio... I have used Praat to visualize the MFA output and found it to be very accurate.

claims.claim: A specific WhisperX alignment failure mode is timestamps that begin in the middle of the first word of a sentence even after long silences, observable by inspecting the audio in Audacity; adjusting models and VAD onset did not fix it, whereas MFA aligned the same problematic sentences much better.
claims.quote: What I seeing (hearing :)) on my end are timestamps starting in the middle of the first word of sentences. In a particular case, the sentence starts after a long period of silence, and timestamp is still way off... I tried different models and also playing with VAD options, like loweing the vad-onset value, but it didn't help... MFA did a much better job on the problematic sentences.

claims.claim: A recommended workaround is to keep WhisperX's VAD and batched Whisper transcription but substitute MFA (or alternatives like CrisperWhisper, or Gentle) for the alignment step, since more accurate aligners exist for the text-to-audio timing map.
claims.quote: Perhaps you can preserve the VAD & batch whisper processing of WhisperX but use MFA for the alignment... You could also experiment with CrisperWhisper

claims.claim: MFA and Gentle (Kaldi-based aligners) are more accurate than WhisperX but have heavy install/dependency burdens (conda/Kaldi builds), and both sometimes replace unrecognized words with an <unk> token while still producing precise timestamps for those segments.
claims.quote: Installing it without conda requires a priest, 10 Buddhists monks, and sacrificing 20 virgins... MFA gave the 1. a very precise timestamp, but replaced the text with <unk>... Decided to give gentle a try and it performed extremely well. Just like MFA, sometimes it does not understand a word and replaces it by <unk>... The biggest problem with gentle is that it also relies on kaldi

claims.claim: The WhisperX-vs-forced-aligner timestamp accuracy gap is corroborated by peer-reviewed research, with commenters citing an Interspeech 2024 paper, indicating this is not an isolated user error.
claims.quote: You are not alone in these results. (See also this Interspeech 2024 paper.)

content.text:
Extracted claims from the WhisperX GitHub issue #1247. Source is a GitHub issue thread (forum-quality: practitioner reports, not formal benchmarks), published 2025-10-03 with 5 corroborating comments through late October 2025.

Key findings relevant to the research question's alignment/timing angle (#2):
- Multiple independent users report WhisperX's wav2vec2 forced-alignment timestamps are materially less accurate than MFA, with characteristic failures like timestamps landing mid-first-word even after long silences, and VAD-onset tuning not helping.
- MFA and Gentle are more accurate but carry heavy install costs (conda / Kaldi builds) and both emit `<unk>` for unrecognized words.
- Recommended hybrid: keep WhisperX VAD + batched Whisper transcription, swap in MFA/Gentle/CrisperWhisper for alignment.
- The gap is corroborated by an Interspeech 2024 paper cited in-thread (raises source credibility beyond pure anecdote).

Caveat: the issue contains no quantified error magnitudes (no millisecond offsets or accuracy percentages) — comparisons are qualitative/visual (Praat, Audacity, an attached comparison image), so treat magnitude claims as directional rather than benchmarked.
